items.myth: Backpropagation is a separate algorithm unrelated to calculus.
items.reality: Backpropagation is the efficient reverse-mode application of the chain rule on a computation graph—pure multivariable calculus organized for computers.
items.example: loss.backward() in PyTorch is chaining ∂L/∂(next tensor) with local ∂(next)/∂(current) at each recorded op.

items.myth: You only need the chain rule for nested formulas, not for matrix layers.
items.reality: A linear layer z = Wx is a vector-valued inner function; the chain rule uses matrix multiplication (Jacobian) on upstream gradients.
items.example: ∂L/∂x = Wᵀ(∂L/∂z) when x feeds into z = Wx—matrix form of the chain rule.

items.myth: Autograd might use numerical approximation instead of the chain rule.
items.reality: Standard reverse-mode autograd applies exact symbolic chain rules on primitives; numerical differentiation is a separate debugging tool.
items.example: torch.autograd.gradcheck compares analytic chain-rule gradients to finite differences for validation, not for training.
